summary: Gruber meticulously unpacks the confusion surrounding the FBI's handling of the San Bernardino shooter's iPhone, specifically the resetting of the Apple ID password. The phone had been configured for iCloud backups but hadn't backed up in six weeks. Apple suggested the FBI could trigger a backup by connecting the phone to a known Wi-Fi network, but the FBI directed the county to reset the Apple ID password, which permanently prevented the phone from initiating a new iCloud backup. Gruber explains the critical distinction between the Apple ID password (which was reset) and the device passcode (which remains unknown). He concludes that the FBI's actions were either incompetent or deliberately designed to create the legal confrontation with Apple over building an iOS backdoor.
key_points: The iPhone's owner was San Bernardino County (Farook's employer), not Farook himself, and the county reset the Apple ID password at the FBI's direction
A locked iPhone can back up to iCloud without being unlocked, but cannot do so if the associated Apple ID password has been changed — the FBI's password reset eliminated this possibility
Apple had already provided the FBI with a six-week-old iCloud backup under warrant and suggested a method to trigger a fresh backup
The FBI's mishandling of the Apple ID password reset points to either incompetence or deliberate manipulation to force a legal precedent for iOS backdoors
iCloud backup data is largely not encrypted in a way Apple can't access, meaning the backup route would have given the FBI what they claimed to want
money_quotes: The only possible explanations for this are incompetence or dishonesty on the part of the FBI.
Dishonesty, if they directed the county to do this knowing the repercussions, with the goal of setting up this fight to force Apple to create a back door for them in iOS.
The key point is that you do not have to unlock an iPhone to have it back up to iCloud. But a locked iPhone can't back up to iCloud if the associated Apple ID password has been changed.
They did not, and apparently could not, change anything on the phone itself.
I'd like to know exactly when this directive to reset the Apple ID password was given — 'in the hours after the attack' leaves a lot of wiggle room.
I would bet a large sum of money that Apple is hard at work on an iCloud backup system that does store data encrypted in a way that Apple cannot read it without the user's Apple ID password.
key_insight: The FBI either bungled the San Bernardino investigation by resetting the Apple ID password and destroying their best path to the iPhone's data, or they did it deliberately to manufacture a legal battle over iOS encryption backdoors.
